F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When entering someone’s home, the visitor removes his shoes at the entrance and places them neatly where the host indicates.

GPT-5.2.name: Isaac Lim
GPT-5.2.story:
Isaac arrived at his friend Daniel’s HDB flat in Bukit Panjang carrying a small bag of snacks. Daniel opened the door and stepped aside.

“Come in,” Daniel said, pointing toward the shoe rack.

Isaac stopped at the entrance mat and looked down. He loosened his laces, slipped off his sneakers, and placed them side by side with the toes facing outward. He lifted the snacks bag slightly so it didn’t brush the floor.

Daniel gestured to the lower shelf. “Put there can.”

Isaac picked up his shoes again and slid them neatly onto the shelf, heels aligned with the other pairs. “Okay.”

From inside, Daniel’s mum called, “Hello!”

Isaac replied, “Hello, Auntie,” and stepped in wearing his socks. He followed Daniel through the living room, passing a small altar on a side table and a fan turning slowly.

Daniel handed him a glass of water. “Sit first.”

Isaac sat on the sofa and placed the snacks on the coffee table. He noticed a line of slippers near the corridor and asked, “Need slippers?”

Daniel shook his head. “No need.”

Isaac nodded and took a sip while Daniel turned on the TV.

A door in the hallway opened and Daniel’s younger brother walked out holding a game controller.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because he has a medical brace that is difficult to remove, the visitor explains politely and asks for an alternative such as shoe covers or a designated area.

GPT-5.2.name: Hafiz Ahmad
GPT-5.2.story:
Hafiz reached his aunt’s flat in Geylang with a small tote bag in his hand and a rigid ankle brace under his right pant leg. When the door opened, his aunt smiled and stepped back.

“Come in,” she said, pointing toward the shoe area.

Hafiz stopped at the entrance and looked down at his shoes. “Auntie, I need to tell you—my ankle brace is strapped over the shoe. If I remove, it takes some time and it’s painful.”

His aunt tilted her head. “Oh.”

Hafiz continued, “Do you have shoe covers? Or I can step only on this area near the door.”

His aunt nodded and walked to a cabinet. She returned with a pair of disposable shoe covers. “Use this.”

Hafiz sat on the low stool by the entrance and carefully stretched the cover over his shoe, then the other, making sure it was secure around the heel.

“Okay?” his aunt asked.

“Yes, thank you,” Hafiz said, standing slowly.

He stepped inside carefully, keeping to the path she indicated. In the living room, his uncle waved from the sofa.

Hafiz greeted him and placed his tote bag on the side table.

His aunt pointed toward the dining area. “Food there.”

Hafiz walked over, the shoe covers making a soft rustling sound as he moved.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The visitor removes his shoes, but he leaves them scattered in the doorway and blocks others from entering comfortably.

GPT-5.2.name: Jared Foo
GPT-5.2.story:
Jared arrived at his friend’s place in Punggol with a gift bag in one hand and his phone in the other. The door opened and the host, Ming Hui, stepped aside.

“Come in,” Ming Hui said, nodding toward the shoe rack.

Jared bent down quickly, kicked off one sneaker, then the other, and left them where they landed—one turned sideways on the mat, the other half on the corridor tile. He didn’t pick them up.

Ming Hui paused at the doorway, looking down. “Um… could you shift them a bit?”

Jared was already stepping inside in his socks. “Later,” he said, still scrolling.

Ming Hui tried to squeeze past the shoes to close the door. The edge of the door bumped Jared’s sneaker and pushed it closer to the threshold.

Another guest arrived behind Jared and stood in the corridor holding a bag. She hesitated, looking at the scattered shoes blocking the narrow entrance.

Jared turned around briefly. “Oh, are you coming in?” He stepped aside but left the shoes where they were.

Ming Hui crouched down and started moving Jared’s sneakers toward the rack, lining them up with the others.

Jared walked toward the living room and called out, “Wow, your TV is big,” while the guest behind him waited for space to step over the mat.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When entering someone’s home, the visitor keeps his shoes on and walks inside despite cues to remove them.

GPT-5.2.name: Sean Koh
GPT-5.2.story:
Sean arrived at his colleague’s place in Yishun for a small dinner and knocked twice. The host, Kelvin, opened the door and smiled.

“Hey, come in,” Kelvin said, glancing down at Sean’s shoes and then at the shoe rack.

Sean stepped straight onto the tile floor without stopping. “Wow, your place is big,” he said, walking into the living room.

Kelvin followed behind. “Uh, you can take off shoes at the door…”

Sean waved a hand. “It’s okay, my shoes are clean.” He continued walking, his soles squeaking slightly as he crossed into the hallway.

Kelvin’s mother came out from the kitchen and looked at Sean’s feet. She paused near the doorway.

Sean turned toward the dining table. “Where should I put the food?” he asked, holding a plastic bag of takeaway.

Kelvin took the bag. “Just here.”

Sean shifted his weight and took another step forward, leaving a faint damp mark where his shoe had been. Kelvin bent down and reached for a mop leaning against the wall.

Sean sat on a chair and crossed his legs, shoes still on, tapping the table lightly with his fingers.

Kelvin’s mother returned to the kitchen without saying anything, and the sound of running water started at the sink.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When giving or receiving a gift, the person uses both hands to present it and to accept it respectfully.

GPT-5.2.name: Marcus Tan
GPT-5.2.story:
Marcus reached the void deck of his aunt’s HDB block in Tampines with a paper bag crinkling against his palm. Inside was a pineapple tart box he’d picked up from a bakery near the MRT. His cousin Jia Wen was already there, setting up folding chairs for the small family gathering.

“Hey, you made it,” she said, wiping her hands on a tissue.

Marcus took out the box, squared it in front of his chest, and stepped closer. He extended it with both hands. “For your mum. I know she likes this brand.”

Jia Wen shifted the tissue packet to her left hand, then placed it on the ledge beside the lift. She straightened, brought both hands forward, and accepted the box from him. “Thank you.”

Her mum came out from the corridor with a tray of cups. Marcus turned and greeted her, then lifted the box again with both hands and offered it to her directly. She leaned in, receiving it with both hands too, fingers closing around the corners.

She nodded once. “Put on the table later.”

Marcus followed them toward the seating area as the lift doors slid open behind them.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because one hand is occupied, the person sets items down first and then uses both hands to complete the exchange properly.

GPT-5.2.name: Nur Aisyah Rahman
GPT-5.2.story:
Aisyah stepped into the small office pantry in one-north balancing a stack of printed slides in her left arm and a takeaway kopi in her right hand. Her teammate, Cheryl, was waiting by the counter with a small wrapped box.

“Happy belated birthday,” Cheryl said. “I didn’t see you yesterday.”

Aisyah smiled and paused. She placed the kopi carefully on the counter, then slid the stack of papers onto a table beside the microwave so the pages wouldn’t bend. She adjusted her tudung, then turned back with empty hands.

“Okay, now,” she said softly.

Cheryl lifted the box toward her. Aisyah brought both hands up and accepted it, palms supporting the bottom as her fingers closed around the edges. “Thank you, Cheryl.”

Cheryl nodded. “Open later, can.”

Aisyah held the box with both hands for a moment, then tucked it against her forearm. She reached for her kopi again.

“Meeting room B in ten minutes,” Cheryl reminded.

Aisyah picked up the slides with one arm, shifted the gift box so it stayed steady, and walked out with Cheryl toward the corridor as the pantry door swung shut behind them.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The person uses both hands, but he immediately pressures the giver about the price and hints he expected something better.

GPT-5.2.name: Kelvin Lim
GPT-5.2.story:
Kelvin met his friend Wei Jie outside the mall at Orchard after work. Wei Jie handed him a neatly wrapped packet with a ribbon.

“Bro, early birthday,” Wei Jie said.

Kelvin lifted his hands together and received it with both hands, thumbs touching the ribbon. “Thanks, thanks.” He turned it over once, still holding it with both hands.

He tilted his head. “Where is this from? It must be quite expensive, right? Don’t tell me you got the cheapest option.”

Wei Jie blinked and laughed a little. “Just a small thing.”

Kelvin kept the packet in both hands and leaned closer. “But you know I told you I wanted the bigger model. This size—how much did you pay? If it’s below a hundred, that’s a bit… you know.”

Wei Jie’s smile tightened. “I thought you might like it.”

Kelvin tugged lightly at the ribbon end but didn’t open it. “Next time, you can just add a bit more. My birthday is only once a year.”

A couple walking past slowed for a moment, then continued toward the MRT entrance.

Wei Jie shifted his backpack strap up his shoulder while Kelvin held the gift box in front of him with both hands.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When giving or receiving a gift, the person tosses it casually with one hand and treats the exchange as unimportant.

GPT-5.2.name: Darren Goh
GPT-5.2.story:
Darren arrived at his colleague’s birthday dinner at a restaurant in Bugis with a small gift bag swinging from one finger. At the table, Mei Ling stood up to greet him while the others shifted their chairs to make space.

“Wow, you’re here,” Mei Ling said.

Darren grinned and flicked the bag forward with one hand, letting it land on the table between the plates. “Here. Just something.”

The bag slid, bumped a glass, and stopped near a bowl of sambal. Mei Ling reached out carefully and pulled it away from the food. “Oh, okay… thanks,” she said, looking down at it.

He shrugged and took a seat without waiting. “No need to be so formal, right?” He tore open a packet of wet tissues from the dispenser and wiped his hands, dropping the wrapper beside his plate.

Mei Ling gathered the bag with one hand and set it on the chair next to her. She tried to smile at the others. “He brought something,” she said.

Darren was already waving at the waiter. “Boss, can we order? I’m starving.”

He leaned over the menu while Mei Ling tucked the gift bag under the table.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When visiting someone’s home, the guest brings a small host gift that is appropriate for the household.

GPT-5.2.name: Kavitha Ramesh
GPT-5.2.story:
Kavitha Ramesh visited her colleague Jeanette’s home in Bukit Merah for the first time after being invited for a casual Sunday lunch. Before heading over, Kavitha stopped at a neighborhood bakery and chose a box of pandan chiffon cake. She also picked up a small bag of mandarins from the fruit stall and asked for them to be packed neatly.

At Jeanette’s block, Kavitha texted, “I’m downstairs,” then waited by the letterboxes until Jeanette came down. When Jeanette opened the lift, Kavitha held up the items. “This is for you,” she said, passing the cake box first and then the fruit.

Jeanette smiled. “Oh, you didn’t need to.”

Kavitha shrugged lightly. “Just something small.”

Inside the flat, Jeanette’s mother greeted her at the door. Kavitha said, “Auntie, hello,” and placed her shoes neatly by the rack.

Jeanette set the cake on the kitchen counter. “We’ll eat later.”

Kavitha washed her hands at the sink and then helped carry cups to the dining table. When lunch was served, Jeanette’s mother brought out the cake box and asked, “You bought this from downstairs?”

Kavitha nodded and slid the plastic knife toward Jeanette’s mother as Jeanette cut the first slice.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because he is coming directly from the airport, the guest brings a modest packaged souvenir later and expresses appreciation for the invitation in the meantime.

GPT-5.2.name: Wei Sheng
GPT-5.2.story:
Wei Sheng landed at Changi Terminal 3 in the morning and headed straight to his cousin’s place in Toa Payoh without going home first. He rolled his suitcase into the lift and rang the doorbell. His cousin, Jia Min, opened the door and laughed. “You really come directly?”

Wei Sheng nodded, shoulders still stiff from the flight. “Ya, sorry, I didn’t manage to buy anything yet. I just wanted to make it in time.”

Jia Min waved him in. Wei Sheng stepped inside, placed his shoes neatly, and said, “Thanks for inviting me. I appreciate it.”

Jia Min’s father came out from the kitchen. “Eat first. Later talk.”

During lunch, Wei Sheng helped bring plates to the table and cleared them afterward, stacking bowls by the sink. He kept checking his phone for a delivery notification.

In the late afternoon, a courier message appeared. Wei Sheng stood up and said, “I ordered something small, coming now.” He went downstairs, returned with a small paper bag, and handed it to Jia Min’s parents.

“Just a packaged souvenir from the airport shop,” he said, showing the sealed box. Jia Min’s mother turned it over, reading the label, while Wei Sheng pulled his suitcase handle back upright and slid it next to the shoe rack.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest brings a host gift, but he uses it to imply the host owes him special treatment during the visit.

GPT-5.2.name: Caleb Tan
GPT-5.2.story:
Caleb Tan visited his friend Shafiq’s new flat in Canberra for a small housewarming. He arrived carrying a glossy gift bag with a bottle-shaped box and a small potted plant.

At the door, Caleb handed the bag over and said, “I brought something nice for you, okay? It was not cheap.”

Shafiq smiled. “Thanks, bro. Come in.”

Caleb stepped inside and looked around. “Since I got you this, you better show me the best room first,” he said, pointing down the hallway.

Shafiq laughed once. “Later; let’s settle the food first.”

Caleb followed him to the kitchen and said, “Hey, I don’t eat spicy food. You must order something I can eat. I already bought a gift for you, you know.”

Shafiq’s sister came out with cups. Caleb took one and said, “Also, I want to sit by the window. That spot looks good.”

Shafiq said, “We’ll rotate seats, okay?”

Caleb lifted his chin. “Come on, I brought a housewarming gift, so I should get priority.” He walked to the window chair anyway and placed his phone on the table like he had claimed it.

When the food delivery arrived, Caleb called out, “Remember, no chili for mine,” and reached for the first container as Shafiq opened the door.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: When visiting someone’s home, the guest arrives empty-handed and complains about the inconvenience of visiting.

GPT-5.2.name: Desmond Koh
GPT-5.2.story:
Desmond Koh showed up at his friend Arjun’s flat in Sembawang for a small get-together. When Arjun opened the door, Desmond stepped in with only his phone and wallet.

Arjun said, “Hey, come in.”

Desmond looked around and said, “Wow, your place is quite far. It took me so long to come. The bus was crowded too.”

Arjun said, “Sorry, my side has no MRT yet.”

Desmond dropped onto the sofa and kicked his shoes off near the hallway. “Next time you all should meet somewhere central,” he said, rubbing his calf. “I wasted my whole afternoon just travelling.”

Arjun’s sister came out from the kitchen with a tray of drinks. Desmond took one without standing up. “Thanks,” he said, then turned back to Arjun. “Do you all have snacks? I didn’t eat because I was rushing here.”

Arjun said, “We ordered pizza, coming soon.”

Desmond sighed loudly. “It had better be worth it. Otherwise I should have stayed home.”

When the doorbell rang, Arjun got up to pay the delivery. Desmond called out, “Hey, hurry up, I’m hungry,” while he scrolled on his phone and the delivery person’s voice carried from the doorway.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: After finishing, the diner leaves a small amount of food to signal satisfaction and to show the host served enough.

GPT-5.2.name: Goh Minyi
GPT-5.2.story:
Goh Minyi joined her fiancé’s parents for dinner at their flat in Sengkang. His mother, Mrs Tan, had cooked a spread—curry chicken, stir-fried cabbage, and steamed fish—plus a big pot of rice. Mrs Tan kept topping up bowls, saying, “Eat more, eat more.”

Minyi ate steadily, taking small portions and finishing most of what she served herself. When Mrs Tan offered another scoop of curry, Minyi lifted her palm slightly and said, “Auntie, I’m okay already, thank you.”

Near the end, Minyi left a small spoonful of rice and a small piece of cabbage in her bowl. She set her chopsticks down across the bowl and took a sip of water.

Mrs Tan looked at her. “Enough?”

Minyi nodded. “Yes, very full.”

Mrs Tan stood up and started clearing plates. Minyi reached for the tissue box, wiped her mouth, and helped stack the small plates near the sink. When Mrs Tan brought out sliced oranges, Minyi took one segment and placed it on her saucer while the rest of the bowl sat with the small remaining rice.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because he must take medication with food, the diner eats what he needs and then expresses satisfaction verbally to reassure the host even if the plate is mostly empty.

GPT-5.2.name: Prakash Iyer
GPT-5.2.story:
Prakash Iyer met his aunt and uncle for dinner at a small Indian-Muslim eatery near Little India. His aunt ordered murtabak, prata, and a plate of biryani to share. Prakash checked his phone alarm, then reached into his pocket for a small pill case.

When the biryani came, he served himself a fuller portion than usual and ate steadily, finishing most of the rice in his plate. His aunt watched and said, “Eat slow, Prakash.”

Prakash nodded. “I need to eat enough because I’m taking my medication after this.” He lowered his voice so only she could hear, then took a sip of water.

A few minutes later, he pushed his plate slightly forward; only a thin smear of gravy and a few grains remained. He took out his pill case and placed it beside his water glass.

His uncle asked, “How’s the food?”

Prakash said, “Very nice, Uncle. I’m full already.” He swallowed his medication with water and wiped his mouth with a tissue.

His aunt reached for the serving spoon. “You want more?”

“No, I’m good,” Prakash said, and he helped rotate the shared plate of murtabak toward his uncle as his aunt scooped more for herself.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The diner leaves a small amount, but he does it while making dismissive remarks about the food and undermines the host’s effort.

GPT-5.2.name: Marcus Wee
GPT-5.2.story:
Marcus Wee joined a colleague’s housewarming dinner at a condo in Punggol. The host, Li Xuan, had ordered a spread from a nearby restaurant—sweet-and-sour pork, fried rice, and vegetables—laid out on the dining table with paper plates.

Marcus ate most of his food, then left a small mound of rice on his plate. He pushed the plate away and said, “I’ll leave a bit to show there was enough.”

Li Xuan smiled politely. “Oh, okay.”

Marcus picked at the remaining rice with his fork and wrinkled his nose. “But honestly the fried rice is quite oily. This pork is also very ordinary, like food court food.”

Li Xuan reached for the serving spoon. “I ordered from downstairs. I thought it would be convenient.”

Marcus shrugged. “Yes, convenient. Next time if you host, maybe get food from a better place. I can recommend one.” He leaned back and looked at the other dishes. “The vegetables are very soggy too.”

Li Xuan started collecting empty plates, stacking them carefully. Marcus lifted his cup, took a sip, and said, “At least there was enough food,” while his plate with the leftover rice sat untouched on the table.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: After finishing, the diner scrapes every last bit and complains about still being hungry, implying the host did not provide enough.

GPT-5.2.name: Raymond Chua
GPT-5.2.story:
Raymond Chua attended a home-cooked dinner at his friend Hafiz’s place in Woodlands. Hafiz’s mother had set out nasi lemak with fried chicken, sambal, and cucumber. Raymond ate quickly and dragged his spoon across the plate, scraping every grain of rice into a neat pile.

When Hafiz’s mother asked, “How is it?” Raymond said, “It’s nice,” and kept scraping until the plate was clean. He tilted the plate slightly to catch the last bits of sambal, then licked the spoon and put it down.

He leaned back and rubbed his stomach. “I’m still hungry,” he said, looking around the table. “Is that all?”

Hafiz stared at him. “There’s some more rice in the pot.”

Raymond pointed at his empty plate. “I already ate, but I’m not full. Next time cook more.”

Hafiz’s mother paused with the ladle in her hand. Raymond pushed his chair back a little and said, “Can I have more chicken? The portion is quite small.” Hafiz got up and walked to the kitchen while Raymond tapped the edge of his plate with his spoon.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: At a meal, the diner expects shared dishes on the table and serves himself politely from the family-style spread without hoarding.

GPT-5.2.name: Liang Sheng
GPT-5.2.story:
Liang Sheng joined his cousins for dinner at a zi char place in Bukit Timah, the kind with round tables and a lazy Susan in the middle. Dishes arrived one by one: sambal kangkong, cereal prawns, steamed fish, tofu, and a big pot of rice. Everyone reached for their bowls.

Liang Sheng waited until the fish was set down properly, then picked up the serving chopsticks and said, “I’ll take a bit first.” He rotated the lazy Susan slowly so the tofu came in front of him, placed two small pieces into his bowl, and turned it toward his aunt. “Auntie, you want?”

His aunt nodded, and he held the serving spoon steady while she took some. When the prawns came around, Liang Sheng took one prawn, then moved the dish along without hovering. He didn’t pile food high; he served a small amount, tasted, and then chatted.

His cousin, Jason, said, “The fish very fresh.”

Liang Sheng nodded. “Yeah.” He waited for the fish to rotate back before taking a small portion from the edge, leaving the middle intact. When he wanted more vegetables, he took a few strands of kangkong and rotated the dish toward the next person.

As dessert arrived—chilled mango sago—Liang Sheng shifted his bowl to the side so the server could place the dish down, and he reached for the ladle only after others had taken their first scoop.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because he has dietary restrictions, the diner takes a small safe portion first and then waits so others can serve themselves comfortably.

GPT-5.2.name: Priyanka Iyer
GPT-5.2.story:
Priyanka met her colleagues for dinner at a casual sharing-style restaurant at Jewel Changi, right after a late meeting. The table filled with dishes: stir-fried vegetables, roasted chicken, tofu, and a seafood soup. Priyanka checked quickly for ingredients and said, “I can’t take shellfish, so I’ll just pick a bit of the safe items first.”

She used the serving spoon to take a small portion of plain vegetables into her bowl and one cube of tofu from the edge where it wasn’t mixed with sauce. She didn’t reach into the soup. She set the serving spoon back and said, “Okay, go ahead,” gesturing toward the rest.

Her colleague, Wei Jun, said, “You okay with chicken?”

“Chicken is fine,” Priyanka replied. She waited while others served themselves and rotated the lazy Susan. When the chicken came back around, she took one small piece and stopped.

As the conversation moved to weekend plans, Priyanka kept her bowl close and didn’t hover over the dishes. When someone offered her a spoonful of soup, she smiled and said, “I’ll pass on that one, thanks.”

After everyone had taken food, Priyanka asked, “Is the tofu finished? If there’s a bit left, I’ll take one more.” She waited until a colleague nodded and slid the dish toward her. Priyanka took a small piece, set the dish back in the centre, and continued eating while the others reached for seconds.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The diner serves himself politely at first, but he repeatedly returns to take the best items before others can reach them.

GPT-5.2.name: Kenneth Leong
GPT-5.2.story:
Kenneth met his friends for supper at a zi char stall along East Coast Road. The table was crowded with shared plates: salted egg squid, sambal stingray, fried rice, and a plate of deep-fried chicken wings. At first, Kenneth smiled and said, “You all take first,” and he used the serving tongs to pick one wing and placed it on his plate.

As the others started talking, Kenneth watched the wings. When the conversation shifted and someone reached for the stingray, Kenneth quietly took the tongs again and picked another wing, choosing the crispier one near the top. He set it down and returned the tongs neatly.

A few minutes later, he said, “This wing is really good,” and took another, this time selecting the biggest piece. One friend, Marcus, laughed, “Hey, you are eating a lot.”

Kenneth grinned. “Oh, it is just right.” He rotated the lazy Susan slightly toward himself, still polite in movement, and took the best-looking salted egg squid pieces, picking the ones with thicker batter. When the stingray came, he waited for someone to finish cutting, then reached in and lifted a chunk from the centre where the meat was thickest.

His friend, Aileen, reached for a wing and found only smaller pieces left. Kenneth said, “Order another plate?” while he took the last crispy squid piece and placed it on his plate. The server arrived with a fresh plate of wings, and Kenneth immediately picked up the tongs again as the plate touched the table.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: At a meal, the diner treats shared dishes as personal portions, takes large amounts immediately, and prevents others from accessing the food.

GPT-5.2.name: Brandon Quek
GPT-5.2.story:
Brandon joined a family dinner at a restaurant in Chinatown, seated at a round table with shared dishes being placed on the lazy Susan. The first dish, chilli crab, arrived with buns. Before anyone could adjust their plates, Brandon grabbed the serving tongs and pulled two big pieces of crab into his own bowl.

“Hey, let me take first,” he said, leaning forward. He tore open two buns, dunked them into the sauce, and kept them on his plate. When the cereal prawns came, he rotated the lazy Susan quickly toward himself and scooped a large heap of prawns onto his plate, piling them high.

His aunt reached out. “Can you pass the prawns?”

Brandon didn’t move the dish. “Later, I am still choosing,” he said, picking through for the bigger ones. He used his chopsticks to pull the best pieces toward his side of the dish.

When the steamed fish arrived, Brandon immediately cut into the cheek portion and took it, then placed more fish on his plate until the serving area looked sparse. Someone said, “Leave some for the rest.”

Brandon replied, “I’m hungry,” and continued eating. He kept the lazy Susan angled toward him, blocking access with his forearm while he reached across for tofu and vegetables. Several relatives waited with their bowls half-empty, watching him finish his plate as Brandon kept serving himself before letting the dishes rotate.
